rationale_for_baseline_tier: Symmetric hand/wrist synovitis with no single septic-looking joint, no tracking erythema and only low-grade fever, precipitated by stopping the usual NSAID, is an inflammatory flare that can be managed at home by restarting the historically effective NSAID with practical support and safety-netting (T2). A prompt in-person joint review and rheumatology referral are arranged, and this is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 for a single fiery joint, red streaks, or high fever/rigors.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint becoming fiery red/hugely swollen, red streaks up the arm, or high fever with shaking chills
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to ED / call for urgent care
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicate septic arthritis or spreading soft-tissue infection.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one joint turns fiery red and hugely swollen, red streaks travel up your arm, or you get a high fever with chills, go to ED straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to take medication or eat/drink safely alone
escalation_edges.rationale: Loss of hand function while isolated risks medication non-administration and poor intake.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't open your medication or manage food and drink, arrange same-day help — a friend, family, or a home service.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with loss of hand function
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange practical support for medication, meals and hydration.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Inflammatory arthritis off its usual NSAID
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Restart the NSAID and ensure continuity of supply to prevent recurrence.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your joints flared because you ran out of your anti-inflammatory — restarting it (with food) should settle things over a day or so. Both hands are affected symmetrically with no single fiery joint, so this looks like a flare, not an infection. Rest your hands, get help opening medication and food, and arrange an in-person joint check and rheumatology referral.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Restart NSAID via prescription and arrange in-person GP review within a few days; use ED for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Use ED/urgent care for a single fiery joint, red streaks or high fever; otherwise manage at home.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the symmetric hand synovitis flare after NSAID cessation, negative septic screen, and the need for a joint exam and rheumatology referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Re-issuing the NSAID with no septic-arthritis/cellulitis safety-net or joint review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving septic joint or spreading cellulitis is missed after a script is simply re-sent.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/000 for a symmetric, afebrile flare, over-medicalising a manageable presentation.
